# Combat

Combat is what happens when two discovered enemy forces occupy the same location on the map.
If one force or both forces are undiscovered, no combat occurs. Two discovered forces may
occupy the same province, but one might be in a fortress which has not yet been breached. When
one army is in a fort and one besieges, the province is owned by the besieging player and the
fort by the besieged. This is **partial ownership** of the province; when the same player owns
both, it is **full ownership**. (Some events cannot happen in a partially owned province.)

Once combat occurs, units move according to their tactical movement allowance on a grid placed
over the map (toggle with `g` or `w`). It is important to understand that battles in Dominions
are not fought to the death. They are fought until one side loses its nerve and routs. This
allows small units of elite troops to hold off masses of undisciplined barbarians.

Basic points about combat:

- Units are deployed on the battlefield corresponding to the Army Setup screen under Position
  squads.
- The attacker is on the left, the defender on the right.
- Units move across the battlefield in accordance with `<set battle orders>`.
- When units move adjacent to an enemy, they halt due to a "zone of control."
- Once units are adjacent, they attack each other every turn.
- The chance of scoring a hit in melee depends on the difference between attack and defense
  values.
- The chance of scoring a hit with missile weapons depends on the number of units in the
  square the missile hits, and how good the target's shield is.
- The chance of doing damage after scoring a hit depends on the attacker's strength and weapon
  damage versus the defender's protection.
- Magic in combat works a lot like missile weapons.
- Morale is calculated for each squad. A squad takes a morale check when it suffers enough
  casualties, and routs when it fails one.
- Battles are fought until one side routs off the map.
- Troops will rout when all of their commanders have been killed or routed. The entire army
  will rout when their side loses 75% of its total hit points.

So if you kill or rout all of an army's commanders, the whole army will rout, no matter how
big it is. (For this reason, there is no "attack commanders" order; you have to figure out how
best to do this yourself.)

## Battles View

When you get a report on a battle, click on the message "There was a battle in `<province>`"
to get the battle summary with casualty lists. Clicking "View battle" takes you into the
battle replay. You can change the level of detail in the upper-left action box by hitting the
number keys: `1` for least detail, `2-3` for more, `4` for most.

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| Arrow | scroll around the map |
| PageUp / PageDown | adjust the camera height |
| Space | pause the action |
| c | toggle colored squares for the teams |
| g | toggle grid |
| w | toggle the wire frame grid (battlefield graphics disappear) |
| q | quit out of the battle replay |
| f | speed up replay to fast speed |
| t | speed up replay to fastest speed |
| s | switch replay back to normal speed |
| v | while viewing a unit, show its combat log |
| z | slow motion replay |
| F1 | list of all the units |
| F2 | view current weather and dominion scales |
| ? | view the available hotkeys |
| 1/2/3/4 | adjust the level of detail in the battle log |
| +/- | scroll the battle log |

: Battle replay keys

## Army Setup

Units in Dominions 6 are placed into squads under the leadership of a commander. A single
commander can lead up to five squads. The total number of units a commander can lead is the
Leadership rating found in the unit abilities.

Squad limits related to leadership values:

- **Leadership 10:** automatic -1 to morale for a single squad. An additional -1 to all squads
  for every squad beyond the first (so five squads = -1 base and -4 for four extra = -5).
- **Leadership 50:** No morale penalty if leading one or two squads. -1 penalty for every
  additional squad.
- **Leadership 100:** +1 morale for all squads if three or fewer. -1 penalty for every squad
  above three.
- **Leadership 150:** +2 morale for up to 4 squads, -1 penalty to all if a fifth squad is
  added.
- **Leadership 200:** +3 morale for all five squads.

The morale effects of leadership rating are based on the base leadership rating of the
commander. Commanders gain more leadership with experience, but the morale bonuses stay the
same.

| Experience level | Leadership bonus | Poor leaders (ldr 10) |
|-----------------:|-----------------:|----------------------:|
| 1 | +25 | +5 |
| 2 | +50 | +10 |
| 3 | +50 | +10 |
| 4 | +50 | +10 |
| 5 | +50 | +10 |

Other effects on morale:

- Mixing undisciplined units with normal makes the entire squad undisciplined and hits them
  with -1 morale.
- Mixing undead with living gets a morale penalty of -1.
- Mixing demons with normal units gets a morale penalty of -1.

Each unit is placed on the map in a grid square. A single grid square can hold a total of ten
size points of units (so three human-sized units of size 3). Unassigned units (those in the
garrison box) are formed into one large squad at the center of their side's army.

### Formations

Formations are the patterns used to deploy units of a squad onto the map. There are five
types, some of which can only be used by good leaders.

**Box**
: Deploys your units in as close to a square as possible. A squad of 36 Infantry of Ulm (size
3) deployed in a box would be placed with four squares frontage and three deep, yielding 12
squares of three units each.

**Line**
: Deploys your units in a straight line facing the enemy. Only available to good commanders.

**Double line**
: The same as a line except half the units are placed in a rank immediately behind the first.
Only available to good commanders.

**Sparse line**
: Identical to a line, except that an empty square is inserted between each square of units.
This doubles the length of the line and carries a -1 penalty to morale. Only available to good
commanders.

**Skirmish**
: A box-like checkerboard formation which separates each square of units from its neighbors
with an empty square in four directions. It has a -1 penalty to morale.

Undisciplined squads automatically use the skirmish formation and cannot use any other. The
ability **Tight Rein** negates the undisciplined trait of a commander's units. The
**Skirmisher** ability negates the -1 morale penalty for skirmish or sparse line formations.
The **Formation Fighter** ability allows you to fit more units in a square.

## Battlefield movement

Each unit in combat has a combat speed. A move of one square costs roughly one point of combat
speed, unless it is diagonal (50% more). When units move adjacent to an enemy, they become
locked in a "zone of control." Fleeing units ignore zones of control.

Usually there can only be 10 size points in a single square. However, giants or other monsters
considerably larger than the other units can enter the square by displacing the smaller units.
The large unit must be at least 3 size points larger than any displaced unit and it costs one
extra point of combat speed. Trampling units will never try to displace anyone as long as they
are not routing.

Each unit moves individually and has a cooldown at the end of each action. A long cooldown is
one round (after a strike); a short cooldown comes after a move. When moving, the cooldown is
mostly determined by the unit's combat move, so faster units wait a shorter time. If two units
are adjacent, the one that finishes its cooldown first strikes next.

## Melee combat

Melee combat is one combatant physically striking another. The attacker makes an attack roll,
the defender makes a defense roll, and these are compared. If the attack roll (with modifiers)
is greater than the defense roll, it hits.

- **Attack roll:** attacker's Attack attribute + DRN - Fatigue penalty
- **Defense roll:** defender's Defense value + DRN - Fatigue penalty

Once a hit is scored, damage is determined:

- **Damage roll:** attacker's Strength attribute + weapon Damage attribute + DRN
- **Protection roll:** defender's Protection attribute + DRN (+ shield Protection if it is a
  "shield hit")

If the defender has a shield, the hit is a shield hit unless the attack beats the combined
value of the target's defense and the shield's Parry value plus the random roll. If a hit is a
shield hit, the shield's Protection is added to the defender's protection roll.

> **Example:** A Heavy Infantry unit has a broad sword, chain mail hauberk, half helmet and
> shield, with base defense 10, +1 from the sword, -2 from the armor, -1 from the shield, and a
> shield Parry value of 4. Actual defense is 8; total defense with parry is 12. It is attacked
> by a Vanjarl of Vanheim (attack 14). The Vanjarl rolls a 6 for a total of 20. If the Heavy
> Infantry rolls 7 or less it is a clean hit; 8-11 is a shield hit; 12 or higher is a miss.

The Protection value used is the target's listed Protection, unless it is a head hit, in which
case the helmet Protection is used (shield Protection is still added on a shield hit). If the
damage roll is greater than the protection roll, the difference is subtracted from hit points.

### Armor defeating hits

A low protection roll may result in an armor defeating hit that bypasses 25% of the protection.
Fatigue plays a large role; units that are immobilized or unconscious count as having 100
fatigue for this purpose.

- Protection roll of 2 always results in an armor defeating hit.
- Protection roll of 3 results in an armor defeating hit against targets with 50+ fatigue.
- Protection roll of 4 results in an armor defeating hit against targets with 100+ fatigue.

### Shield destruction

Shield hits can damage or destroy the shield. A shield has a Resistance (its shield protection
value, +5 if magic). The attack has a Break value (its damage before any Protection; slashing
adds 50%, blunt adds 25%).

- If the Break value is >= 3x the Shield Resistance, the shield is damaged.
- If the Break value is >= 5x the Shield Resistance, the shield is broken.
- An already-damaged shield which is damaged again has a 25% chance of breaking.
- A damaged shield has -20% Protection; a broken shield has -50% Protection.

A magic shield that is damaged repairs itself after battle; if broken, it is permanently
destroyed. Damaged or broken mundane shields are repaired in a province with enough spare
production resources.

### Hit locations

An attack has a 50% chance of striking the torso, 20% the arms, 20% the legs, and 10% the
head. Certain hits can cause afflictions, such as losing the limb that was hit. Losing your
head is usually instant death (with exceptions like undead and multi-headed creatures).

To score a hit on a body part, the attacker must be able to reach it. To score a head hit,
attacker size + weapon length must equal target size; one less to hit the torso, two less for
the arms. Thus a human (size 3) wielding a mace (length 1) could hit a size-7 creature only in
the legs. Some monsters have their heads located lower (lizards and dragons are two steps
easier to hit above the legs; most four-footed beings are one step easier). Some monsters lack
hit locations (a dragon has no arms; a gelatinous cube has no arms, legs, or head). A
significantly larger unit (2+ sizes larger) attacking a smaller one has an increased chance of
a head hit and lower chance of a leg hit (20% head, 10% leg).

## Weapon types

Melee weapons come in three types: slashing, piercing, and blunt.

- **Blunt** weapons do 25% more damage on head hits (before Protection is deducted) and 25%
  more toward shield destruction.
- **Slashing** weapons do 25% more damage after Protection is deducted, and 50% more toward
  shield destruction. A slashing leg/arm/head hit costing >= 50% of the target's hit points
  chops off that body part.
- **Piercing** weapons reduce Protection by 15% prior to any calculation. (The Armor-Piercing
  ability reduces Protection by 50% and stacks with piercing for 65% total.)
- **Two-handed** weapons add 125% of Strength to damage.

A weapon may inflict more than one type of damage, with an equal chance of doing each but only
one per attack (e.g. the short sword does slashing or piercing, never both). Weapon types apply
to natural weapons too (a spider's fangs do piercing damage).

**Underwater effects:** Slashing and blunt weapons have an attack penalty equal to weapon
length underwater, while piercing weapons do not. If a weapon does both piercing and another
type, the underwater penalty is halved. Flails have an additional penalty of -1.

Damage is displayed in colored numbers above the unit: pale red is normal damage, dark red is
bleeding, green is poison, etc.

## Harassed

Every time a unit is attacked, it gets a point of harassment penalty, each reducing the unit's
defense by 1. It is then reduced continuously by a percentage. For cavalry units only the unit
attacked (rider or horse) is affected. A weapon with multiple attacks inflicts 1 point of
harassment per attack. Only directed melee attacks cause harassment; ranged and AoE attacks do
not.

## Fatigue

Fatigue is affected by a unit's encumbrance value. Each time a unit attacks, it gains Fatigue
equal to its current Encumbrance value.

- **Defense penalty:** for each 10 points of fatigue (rounded down) a unit has its defense
  reduced by 1.
- **Attack penalty:** for each 20 points of fatigue (rounded down) a unit has its attack
  reduced by 1.
- **Unconsciousness:** a unit falls unconscious at 100 fatigue, regaining 5 fatigue per turn
  until it drops below 100. A unit at 200 fatigue starts taking hit point damage from
  exhaustion instead of fatigue.

## Repel

Repel represents the effectiveness of long weapons (halberds, pikes, etc.). Any defender with
a longer weapon than its attacker makes a repel attack automatically upon being attacked. If
the result is a hit, the attacker must pass a repel morale check or immediately abort his
attack.

- **Attacker morale check:** morale + DRN - (weapon length difference)
- **Repeller check:** 10 + DRN + (the number by which the defender won the repel attack) / 2

Units with low morale are more likely to be repelled, so using long weapons against
low-morale troops is very effective. Units with claws and bites (weapon length zero) are
easier to repel. The repelling unit gets a lingering -2 to its Repel roll, gradually removed,
so it's harder to repel attacks in quick succession. Giant-sized units (size 6+) wield weapons
1 point longer than ordinary human weapons.

## Multiple attacks and weapons

Some units have multiple attacks (multiple weapons, or biting/clawing/tentacles). Multiple
attacks can target different units in reach. Some weapons (e.g. flail) have more than one
attack that always target the same unit.

Attackers with multiple weapons have their attack skill reduced by the sum of their weapon
lengths, so it is much easier to fight with two daggers than two swords. The Ambidextrous
ability reduces this penalty. Some natural weapons are "bonus weapons" (e.g. a lizardman's bite
alongside a trident) and cause no multiple-weapon penalty. Units with multiple wielded weapons
get one point of extra encumbrance for each weapon after the first.

### Melee attack resolution order

1. **Determine target** -- which unit (or mount) in the square is targeted.
2. **Early strikeback effects** -- longer-range defensive abilities (Awe, the Gorgon's
   petrifying gaze). If the attacker is immobilized, the attack aborts.
3. **Repel** -- check for repel; abort if repelled.
4. **Strikeback effect** -- Slimer, Sight Vengeance, Horror Mark Attacker, etc. Abort if the
   attacker is immobilized.
5. **Attack and Defence rolls** -- abort if the attack missed completely.
6. **Damage and Protection rolls** -- shield protection added if a shield hit was indicated.
7. **Defensive abilities** -- Mirror Image, Protective Force, Luck, Mossbody, etc. may reduce
   damage or negate the attack.
8. **Limb cap** -- a hit to an arm or leg (or head for certain beings) is limited to at most
   half of max HP.
9. **Damage Redistribution** -- Damage Reversal, Blood Bond, or Blood Vengeance may shift
   damage or kill the attacker (without negating the attack).
10. **Deal damage** -- to the target and any shields that were hit.

### Dancing Weapons

Some magic items (Dancing Trident, Stone Birds) float around their wielder and attack nearby
enemies independently, with no multiple-weapon penalty or fatigue. They cause harass penalty,
cannot be repelled, but can repel attacks aimed at their owner if longer than the attacking
weapon.

## Mounted Units

Some units (cavalry, chariots) are formed from a rider and a mount combined as a single
entity. The parts can be targeted and wounded separately; if one dies the other remains. A
horse without a rider routs, but some monsters (e.g. the gryphon) stay and fight.

### Hitting mounted units

When a mounted unit is attacked, either the rider or the mount is targeted, based on the
difference of their sizes and the reach of the attacker (size + weapon length). Mounts much
larger than their rider have an extra chance of being hit:

| Size difference | Chance of targeting mount |
|----------------:|--------------------------:|
| 3 | 25% |
| 4 | 50% |
| 5+ | 75% |

If the mount wasn't large enough or the attack didn't target it, the attack chooses a target
as follows:

- Missile attacks have a 50% chance of targeting the mount.
- If the mount's size is 2 larger than the reach, the mount is targeted.
- Otherwise chance of targeting the mount is 30% + 10% * mount's size - 10% * reach (minimum
  10%).
- All AoE attacks and Lightning target both rider and mount. Trample always targets the mount.

### Mounts, items, and morale

A single magic saddle or barding can be put on a commander's mount. Some of the rider's items
also bestow effects on the mount (indicated in the item info). Using two-handed weapons while
mounted gives a -3 attack penalty. The **Skilled Rider** ability increases the Morale and
Defence Skill of the mount (the defence increase is reduced by the mount's armor encumbrance).

Unnatural fear effects usually target both rider and mount. If the rider is routed he leaves
with his mount. If only the mount is broken, the rider is thrown to the ground (and the mount
routs) but fights on. Falling off a mount deals a size-difference open-ended roll of armor
negating damage (falling off a chariot does no fall damage). Normal rout checks affecting the
squad only affect the rider.

### Re-recruiting mounted units

When a rider loses his mount he claims a riderless surviving mount of the appropriate type, or
else leaves the army and returns to his home province (rolling morale checks every turn; a
failed check means desertion or death). If he arrives home, a new mounted unit can be recruited
at half cost, retaining his identity, experience, and afflictions (shown by a small blue number
in the recruitment screen). Normal mounts don't return home, but **Smart Mounts** do. Mounted
commanders left without a mount don't return home; they claim a regular unit's mount or use the
**Reclaim Mount** order.

## Missile combat

Missile combat does not use defense values. Instead, the game determines which square a missile
hits, depending on the unit's Precision and the range. Missiles can't distinguish friend from
foe. If the range from attacker to target is greater than Precision/2 - 2, the missile
deviates; the amount of deviation is range x 1.25 / Precision. The distribution is a bell
curve.

Once the game decides where a missile lands, any unit in that square may be targeted, weighted
by relative size (a giant of size 6 sharing a square with a human of size 3 is struck two times
out of three). The hit calculation uses:

- **Attacker:** DRN + (Size points in the square)/2 + 2 if magic weapon
- **Defender:** DRN + (shield parry value x2) - (Fatigue / 20)

If the attacker's roll is greater, a hit is achieved. Damage is calculated as in melee. Most
missile weapons add one-half of the unit's Strength to the weapon damage value. Crossbows and
some weapons are armor-piercing (only half the defender's Protection used); some spells are
armor-negating (armor affords no protection). Lightning spells are armor-negating; fire spells
are armor-piercing. Precision values greater than 10 count double for the amount above 10 (so
Precision 12 is calculated as 14).

## Special damage

Some types of damage cause additional damage, inflict damage gradually, or increase fatigue
instead of reducing hit points: fire, cold, poison, fatigue, paralysis, and drain.
**Resistances** work like protection, but damage is also reduced by a percentage after the
resistance is deducted (the reduction is twice the resistance value, so fire resistance 50 is
complete immunity).

> **Example:** A Pillar of Fire spell with 35 fire damage hits an unarmored unit with 15 fire
> resistance and protection 10 armor. The damage DRN is 11, total 46 fire damage. Pillar of
> Fire is armor-piercing so armor gives protection 5, but fire resistance gives its full 15.
> The protection DRN is 6, total protection 26. The difference is 20, reduced by 30% (twice the
> fire resistance), so the target suffers 14 points of damage.

Elemental resistances give double protection against fatigue-causing attacks (Shock Resistance
5 reduces thunder fatigue by 10).

### Fire damage

Fire is generally armor-piercing. A unit may catch fire and burn until it goes out (fire
fatigue damage only counts as 1/3 toward catching fire).

- **Chance of catching fire:** Fire damage before protection x 4%
- A burning unit takes (1d(size))/2 damage each turn (halved for units with 5+ Fire
  Resistance).
- **Chance of putting out fire:** 25% + (fire resistance) + (cold scale x5) + (100% if
  raining), minimum 1%.

Fire vulnerability counts as negative fire resistance. Units with 10+ fire resistance, chill
auras, mistform, or ethereal units cannot burn. Heat scales count as negative cold.

### Cold damage

A unit suffering cold damage may freeze, taking additional fatigue until it thaws.

- A freezing unit takes 2d6 additional fatigue per turn until it thaws.
- **Chance of thawing:** 25% + (cold resistance) + (heat scale x5), minimum 1%.

Cold vulnerability counts as negative cold resistance. Units with 10+ cold resistance, heat
auras, or ethereal units never freeze.

### Profuse bleeding

A bleeding unit suffers 10 fatigue and takes HP/20 damage each round.

- **Chance to stop bleeding:** 10% + (regeneration value), halved when fighting underwater.

### Poison damage

Poison damage is suffered over multiple rounds (10% of the remaining damage each round). A
poisoned unit also has reduced attack and defence skill: 25% per dose equal to the unit's full
HP, capped at 50%. Poison resistance only reduces the initial total damage, not the duration.

> **Example:** A unit with 10 HP takes 15 points of poison: 2,2,2,1,1,1,1,1,1,1 over ten rounds.
> Its attack and defence are reduced by 37.5% when poisoned, decreasing over time.

### Shock, acid, life drain, paralysis, fatigue

- **Shock damage** can Stun a target. **Chance of getting stunned:** 5% + (% of hit points lost
  from this hit) / 2. Stun prevents any action for one round.
- **Acid damage** can inflict Rust. **Chance of equipment rusting:** Acid damage before
  protection x 4% (only iron weapons/armor). Rusty armor has a (damage before armor protection,
  after shield) x 2% chance of being damaged when hit. Rusty weapons have a 25% chance of being
  damaged on a hit. Damaged weapons do -2 damage (-1 for blunt).
- **Life Drain** restores the attacker's hit points (damage / 2) and reduces fatigue (damage x
  2). Partial life drain only treats the first 5 points of damage as drain. Life drain cannot
  raise HP above 150% of maximum +10. Lifeless units take only 25% damage and never heal an
  attacker.
- **Paralysis** duration = (Damage - victim's Size) / 2. If already paralyzed, additional
  damage = (minimum of old and new duration) / 2, never exceeding 5 points; the new duration is
  the greater of old and new (not cumulative).
- **Fatigue damage** adds to fatigue rather than subtracting hit points. No unit can exceed 200
  fatigue; each 50 fatigue points inflicted past 200 inflicts 1 hit point of damage. Below 50,
  the chance of 1 hit point of damage is (fatigue points x 2)%.

### False damage

Illusions and most Glamour spells cause only false damage, kept real in the victims' minds by
glamour magic. If all enemy glamour mages die, false damage dissipates quickly (2 points/round)
without scars. Damage + false damage equaling total HP kills instantly. False damage never
causes afflictions (apart from death), does not trigger Damage Reversal/Blood
Vengeance/Blood Bond, and cannot be healed by regeneration, life drain, or healing spells. It
is shown as light purple numbers.

## Clouds

Certain beings, spells, and attacks create battlefield clouds (a Hydra's poison cloud, a
Fireball's lingering heat cloud). Clouds dissipate quickly if not replenished (about 1 level
per round). Multiple clouds of the same type cannot overlap; instead they spread further or
raise a square by at most one level. **Damage from clouds is always armor negating.** All
clouds range from level 1 to 7. Monsters that spread clouds spread level 1 clouds (accumulating
to at most level 2); spells and items can create higher levels. Heat Clouds and Frost Clouds
cancel each other out.

| Cloud | Effects by level |
|-------|------------------|
| Frost Cloud | L1: 2 fatigue; L2: 3 fatigue; L3: 4 fatigue, 1 damage; L4: 5 fatigue, 2 damage; L5+: 6+ fatigue, 3+ damage (damage capped to 1 unless frozen) |
| Heat Cloud | L1: 2 fatigue; L2: 3 fatigue; L3: 4 fatigue, 1 damage; L4: 5 fatigue, 2 damage; L5+: 6+ fatigue, 3+ damage |
| Poison Cloud | L1: 1 poison; L2: 2 poison; L3+: 3+ poison |
| Disease Cloud | L1-2: disease (easy MR); L3+: disease (MR) |
| Sulphur Cloud | L1-2: 1 fire fatigue + 1 poison; L3: 2 fire fatigue + 1 poison; L4: 2 + 2 poison; L5: 3 + 2 poison; L6: 3 + 3 poison; L7: 4 + 3 poison (poison capped to 1 damage) |
| Rust Cloud | all levels: armor rusts |
| Death Cloud | L1: 1 damage (MR); L2: 2 damage (MR); L3+: 3+ damage (MR) |
| Shimmering Light Cloud | L1: 1 capped false damage (easy MR); L2: 1 capped false damage (MR); L3: 2 false damage (MR); L4+: 3+ false damage (MR) |
| Leeching Cloud | L1: weakness (easy MR); L2: weakness + 1 damage (easy MR); L3: weakness (MR) + 1 damage (easy MR); L4+: weakness (MR) + 1 damage (MR) -- weakness decreases Str by 1 permanently, damage capped to 1 |
| Sleep Cloud | L1: sleep (easy MR); L2+: sleep (MR) |

: Cloud effects

## Morale and rout

Morale measures how likely a squad is to keep fighting after taking casualties. Each squad has
a morale level, which is the average of all the morale ratings of its members.

A unit can gain morale bonuses:

- +1 for fighting in friendly dominion
- +1 for fighting in a unit's home province
- +x for a commander's leadership bonus (modified by formation, number of squads, etc.)
- +1 for every level of the Inspirational ability the squad's commander has (can be negative)
- +x for a unit with the Standard ability in the squad (x is the highest Standard bonus)

**Morale magic bonus:** many magic effects modify a unit's magic bonus to morale (-10 to +1).
Fear lowers it; priests raise it with Sermon of Courage and Fanaticism. A negative bonus
increases by 1 about every 2 rounds; a positive bonus lasts the entire battle.

### Rout

A squad which fails its morale check routs (the entire squad). A squad checks morale if:

- It has suffered "heavy losses" since the last check, and its overall casualties are at least
  20%.
- It has four or fewer units left and at least one took damage this round.
- It is near a monster causing Fear (e.g. an Abomination).
- It has been subjected to a spell causing Fear (e.g. Terror). Spells causing Frighten do not
  apply.
- The entire army has taken 50% or more of its total hit point value in casualties (then morale
  is checked every turn).

"Heavy losses" is one wound for every two members of the squad. A "wound" is 1+ hit points of
damage that reduces a unit to 80% or less of its normal hit points. A squad cannot have its
morale checked more than once a turn.

**Army rout:** once an army has taken 75% of its total hit point value in casualties, it
automatically routs. Mounts, Province Defence units count only 25% of their HP toward the
army's total; slaves count 50%.

**Morale check:** consists of a morale roll and a fear roll. If the fear roll is greater, the
squad routs.

- **Morale roll:** squad morale + DRN + survivor bonus
- **Fear roll:** 14 + DRN

The survivor bonus ranges from 0-5 depending on how many of the squad's original members are
alive (an intact squad gets 5; one almost wiped out gets 0).

**Rout effects:** a routed unit uses all its action points each turn to move toward its own map
edge, suffering a -4 defense penalty. Once it reaches the edge it is safe and appears in an
adjacent province after the battle. If a unit retreats to an enemy province, it is destroyed.
Routed units suffering bleeding, poison, burning, or decay have the remainder of that damage
applied once off the map and may not survive.

**Mindless units** need a commander. If all eligible commanders are killed or routed, mindless
beings become immobile and have a 33% chance each turn of dissolving and vanishing (they still
attack adjacent units). **Magic beings and undead** rout if on the battlefield without proper
leadership.

**Magic fear effects:** there are two types. **Fear** (the most severe and common) can decrease
morale by up to 10 points and forces the squad to make a morale check at the end of the round;
units may also break individually if they fail an easy morale check. **Frighten** is much less
severe (up to 5 points) and forces no automatic morale checks.

## Long lasting battles and Twilight

If a battle goes on too long the sun sets, with profound effects: battle enchantments end,
berserking ends, and the battlefield is affected by Twilight.

| Turn | Effects |
|-----:|---------|
| 100 | Twilight: all enchantments, temporary magic effects and berserking end. Twilight effect (-2 prec, +1 glamour magic). |
| 150 | Nightfall/Daybreak: attacking units rout. Darkness effect (-3 att/def/prec), or return to daylight in a night-time battle. |
| 170 | Defending units rout. |
| 200 | Any units left on the battlefield are killed. |

Mindless units cannot rout and will dissolve instead.

## Afflictions

Whenever a unit is struck in battle, it may suffer an affliction. The chance is the % of hit
points lost in the blow. It is possible to get more than one if the damage exceeds 100%. There
are major and minor afflictions; the chance of an affliction being major is affliction chance /
1.5, or at most 33%. Where the hit occurs determines which kind may be inflicted.

| Body Part | Minor Affliction | Major Affliction |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Any | Battle Fright | Profuse Bleeding |
| Head | Eye Loss, Dementia, Mute | Feeblemindedness, Blindness |
| Chest | Chest Wound | Diseased, Never-Healing Wound |
| Arm | Weakened | Lost an arm |
| Leg | Limp | Crippled |

Profuse Bleeding is the only non-permanent affliction (it disappears during or after battle).
While bleeding, a unit loses 5% of its hit points and gains 10 fatigue per round, with a 10%
chance of stopping per round (halved underwater, increased by regeneration).

- **Limp** gives commanders a -4 map movement penalty. Non-commander units have a 25% chance of
  becoming crippled whenever their army marches more than one province in a month.
- **Cripple** makes commanders lose 75% of their map movement. Non-commander units have a 35%
  chance of dying when their army marches (70% if marching more than one province).

## Sieges

Fortresses must be reduced before they can be stormed: the Defense value must be reduced to
zero. Each turn, the total reduction strength of the besiegers is compared to the total repair
strength of the defenders.

- **Reduction strength** = unit strength squared (flying units doubled)
- **Repair strength** = unit strength squared / 2 (flying units doubled; mindless units worth
  1/8; animals (not monkeys) halved; undisciplined units halved)

The difference is subtracted from the fortress defense value if reduction is greater. If repair
is greater, the difference is added back (up to the original value). Fortresses which are
damaged but not besieged are automatically fully repaired.

**Supply during sieges:** a besieged fortress provides its supply value divided by the number
of turns of the siege (300, then 150, 100, 75, 60...). Unsupplied units suffer starvation and
may become diseased after two consecutive turns.

**Storming a castle:** when the wall integrity is reduced to zero, besieging commanders can
choose `Storm Castle` or `Maintain Siege`. Storming initiates a battle at the gates: defenders
inside the walls, storming commanders and their units outside. The storming takes place after
any other battle in the province, so a relieving army can fight off the besiegers first. All
forts have additional defending units (Castle Guards and Wall Defenders).

## Weather

Land battles are usually clear and during the day, but can be rainy or snowy at random (unless
in a wasteland or cave). Snowfall requires cold scale 1+; rain requires heat 0 or greater.

- **Snowfall chance:** Cold scale * 15%
- **Rainfall chance:** Growth scale * 10% + 10%

Global enchantments can affect the weather. Assassination battles are special, often indoors
(no weather) with about a 50% chance of occurring at night.

## Retreats

If units retreat from a battle, they go to an adjacent province, or a friendly castle in the
same province. Each commander has a 75% chance of making a "smart" retreat (with a second 50%
chance in native terrain).

- A **smart leader** retreats into a fort in the same province if there is one, or moves to a
  random friendly-controlled adjacent province.
- A **not-so-smart leader** moves into a random adjacent province, even if not friendly
  controlled.

Troops follow a leader when they retreat if they pass a morale check (the squad morale bonus
counts double; undisciplined units suffer -3; the skirmish formation penalty applies). Troops
that lose their leader or fail the check individually check for being smart (50% chance) and
retreat on their own.
